concepts.lesson: The uppermost layer over the earth's surface is called the crust. It is the thinnest of all the layers. It is about 35 km on the continental masses and only 5 km on the ocean floors. The main mineral constituents of the continental mass are silica and alumina (Sial), while the oceanic crust mainly consists of silica and magnesium (Sima).
concepts.real_example: When we dig the ground to build a house, we are interacting with the earth's crust.

concepts.lesson: Just beneath the crust is the mantle which extends up to a depth of 2900 km. The innermost layer is the core with a radius of about 3500 km. It is mainly made up of nickel and iron and is called 'nife'. The central core has very high temperature and pressure.
concepts.real_example: The heat from the core is the energy source that eventually causes volcanoes to erupt.
key_points: Core has extreme temperature and pressure

concepts.lesson: When the molten magma cools, it becomes solid. Rocks thus formed are called igneous rocks. They are also called primary rocks. There are two types: intrusive rocks (cool inside the earth) and extrusive rocks (cool on the surface).
concepts.real_example: Basalt (found in the Deccan plateau) and Granite are common examples of igneous rocks.

concepts.lesson: Rocks roll down, crack, and hit each other and are broken down into small fragments called sediments. These sediments are transported and deposited by wind and water. These compressed and hardened layers of sediments form sedimentary rocks. These rocks may also contain fossils.
concepts.real_example: Sandstone is made from grains of sand compressed over millions of years.
key_points: Formed from fragments of older rocks
Often contain fossils of plants and animals

concepts.lesson: Igneous and sedimentary rocks can change into metamorphic rocks under great heat and pressure. The word 'metamorphic' means 'change of form'. This process takes a very long time and completely alters the appearance and properties of the original rock.
concepts.real_example: Clay changes into slate and limestone changes into marble.
key_points: Formed under high heat and pressure
Results in a change of original form

concepts.lesson: The process of transformation of the rock from one to another is known as the rock cycle. Magma cools to form Igneous rocks; these break into Sedimentary rocks; under heat and pressure, these change into Metamorphic rocks. When metamorphic rocks melt, they turn back into magma, starting the cycle again.
concepts.real_example: A piece of granite breaking down into sand, eventually becoming sandstone, then marble, and finally melting back into magma.
key_points: Magma is the starting and ending point

concepts.lesson: Rocks are made up of different minerals. Minerals are naturally occurring substances which have certain physical properties and definite chemical composition. They are very important to humankind as they are used as fuels, in industries, and in medicine.
concepts.real_example: Coal, natural gas, and petroleum are minerals used as fuels.
key_points: Essential for energy, industry, and health

concepts.lesson: Fossils are the remains of the dead plants and animals trapped in the layers of rocks. They are primarily found in sedimentary rocks because the process of formation is gentle enough to preserve them. Fossils help scientists understand the history of life on Earth and the age of rock layers.
concepts.real_example: Finding a leaf imprint or a dinosaur bone inside a layer of limestone.
